title: Find minimum difference pair after sorting
description: Find pair with minimum difference in sorted array

examples.explanation: Array: [1, 3, 6, 10, 15], n=5. Initialize min_diff = inf. Check pairs: (1,3): diff=2, min_diff=2. (1,6): diff=5, min_diff=2. (1,10): diff=9, min_diff=2. (1,15): diff=14, min_diff=2. (3,6): diff=3, min_diff=2. (3,10): diff=7, min_diff=2. (3,15): diff=12, min_diff=2. (6,10): diff=4, min_diff=2. (6,15): diff=9, min_diff=2. (10,15): diff=5, min_diff=2. Total pairs checked: 10. Return min_diff=2.

Array: [5, 5, 5, 5], n=4. Initialize min_diff = inf. Check pairs: (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. Total pairs checked: 6. Return min_diff=0.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Compare every possible pair of elements in the array to find the minimum difference. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Minimum Difference Pair in Sorted Array Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution comparing all possible pairs to find the minimum absolute difference.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to find the minimum absolute difference between any two elements in a sorted array. The intuition is straightforward: since we need to find the smallest gap between any two numbers, we can simply compare every possible pair of elements. The array being sorted doesn't help the brute force approach since we still check all pairs, but it's mentioned in the problem statement as context. The algorithm works by initializing min_diff to a large value (like infinity), then using nested loops: the outer loop picks the first element of each pair, and the inner loop picks all subsequent elements. For each pair (arr[i], arr[j]) where i < j, we compute the absolute difference |arr[i] - arr[j]| and update min_diff if this difference is smaller than the current minimum. After checking all pairs, min_diff contains the answer. Time complexity is O(n²) because we have n*(n-1)/2 pairs to check. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to L1 as L1 implements the core pair-checking logic. Common mistakes include: not initializing min_diff properly (should be a large number), using wrong loop bounds (inner loop should start from i+1 to avoid redundant checks and self-pairs), forgetting absolute value, and not handling edge cases like single-element arrays (though problem implies at least two elements). Real code pattern: initialize min_diff = float('inf'), for i in range(n): for j in range(i+1, n): diff = abs(arr[i] - arr[j]), if diff < min_diff: min_diff = diff, return min_diff.

examples.trace: Array: [1, 3, 6, 10, 15], n=5. Initialize min_diff = inf. Check pairs: (1,3): diff=2, min_diff=2. (1,6): diff=5, min_diff=2. (1,10): diff=9, min_diff=2. (1,15): diff=14, min_diff=2. (3,6): diff=3, min_diff=2. (3,10): diff=7, min_diff=2. (3,15): diff=12, min_diff=2. (6,10): diff=4, min_diff=2. (6,15): diff=9, min_diff=2. (10,15): diff=5, min_diff=2. Total pairs checked: 10. Return min_diff=2.

Array: [5, 5, 5, 5], n=4. Initialize min_diff = inf. Check pairs: (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. (5,5): diff=0, min_diff=0. Total pairs checked: 6. Return min_diff=0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Compare All Pairs to Find Minimum Value
ladders.desc: Implement nested loops to compare every pair and track the minimum difference.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the brute force approach: comparing every possible pair of elements in an array to find the minimum absolute difference. The intuition is that to find the smallest gap, we must examine all gaps, so we systematically generate all unique pairs (i,j) where i < j. The algorithm uses two nested loops: the outer loop variable i goes from 0 to n-2, and the inner loop variable j goes from i+1 to n-1. For each pair (arr[i], arr[j]), we compute the absolute difference and update a running minimum. This ensures we check exactly n*(n-1)/2 pairs, covering all combinations without repetition. Time complexity is O(n²) due to the nested loops. Space complexity is O(1) as we only store min_diff and loop variables. This connects to L0 as it's the main logic of the full solution, and to L2 which focuses on the pair generation pattern. Common mistakes include: starting inner loop from 0 instead of i+1 (which checks redundant pairs and self-pairs), forgetting to use absolute value (though with sorted array differences are non-negative, but absolute ensures correctness), and not initializing min_diff to a sufficiently large value. Real code pattern: min_diff = float('inf'), for i in range(len(arr)-1): for j in range(i+1, len(arr)): diff = abs(arr[i] - arr[j]), if diff < min_diff: min_diff = diff.

examples.trace: Array: [10, 20, 30, 40], n=4. Initialize min_diff = inf. i=0: j=1: diff=|10-20|=10, min_diff=10. j=2: diff=|10-30|=20, min_diff=10. j=3: diff=|10-40|=30, min_diff=10. i=1: j=2: diff=|20-30|=10, min_diff=10. j=3: diff=|20-40|=20, min_diff=10. i=2: j=3: diff=|30-40|=10, min_diff=10. Total pairs checked: 6. Return min_diff=10.
Array: [1, 4, 7], n=3. Initialize min_diff = inf. i=0: j=1: diff=|1-4|=3, min_diff=3. j=2: diff=|1-7|=6, min_diff=3. i=1: j=2: diff=|4-7|=3, min_diff=3. Total pairs checked: 3. Return min_diff=3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generate All Unique Pairs Using Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested loops with proper indices to generate all (i,j) pairs where i < j.
ladders.explanation: This core logic focuses on the pattern of generating all unique pairs of indices from an array. The intuition is that to compare every element with every other element exactly once, we need a systematic way to avoid duplicates like (i,j) and (j,i). The algorithm uses a nested loop structure where the outer loop index i runs from 0 to n-2, and for each i, the inner loop index j runs from i+1 to n-1. This ensures that for any pair (i,j), we always have i < j, so each unordered pair is considered exactly once. The total number of pairs is the combination C(n,2) = n*(n-1)/2. This is a fundamental pattern in brute force algorithms for pair-related problems. Time complexity is O(n²) as the inner loop runs approximately n times for each i. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as it's the mechanism for pair generation in the sub-routine, and to L3 which deals with loop control. Common mistakes include: running inner loop from 0 to n-1 (which checks n² pairs including duplicates and self-pairs), using wrong bounds (e.g., i up to n-1 causes index error in inner loop), or not understanding why i < j is sufficient (since difference is absolute, order doesn't matter). Real code pattern: for i in range(n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): # process pair (arr[i], arr[j]).

examples.trace: For n=4: i from 0 to 2. i=0: j from 1 to 3: pairs (0,1),(0,2),(0,3). i=1: j from 2 to 3: pairs (1,2),(1,3). i=2: j from 3 to 3: pair (2,3). Total pairs: 6.
For n=3: i from 0 to 1. i=0: j from 1 to 2: pairs (0,1),(0,2). i=1: j from 2 to 2: pair (1,2). Total pairs: 3.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Through Array with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested for-loops to access array elements with controlled indices.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to use nested loops to traverse an array, focusing on index control. The intuition is that many algorithms require comparing each element with others, which naturally leads to using two loops: one to pick a reference element, and another to scan the remaining elements. The algorithm involves an outer loop that sets an index i from 0 to n-2, and for each i, an inner loop sets j from i+1 to n-1. This allows us to access arr[i] and arr[j] in each iteration. It's crucial to understand loop bounds: i goes to n-2 because when i is n-1, there are no j > i. Similarly, j starts at i+1 to avoid comparing an element with itself and to prevent redundant comparisons. Time complexity for traversing all pairs is O(n²). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as it implements the pair generation, and to L4 which covers basic loop operations. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop ranges (e.g., using range(n) for both loops), confusion between zero-based and one-based indexing, and not realizing that inner loop depends on outer loop variable. Real code pattern: n = len(arr), for i in range(n-1): for j in range(i+1, n): # use arr[i] and arr[j].

examples.trace: Array indices: 0:a,1:b,2:c,3:d. Outer i=0: inner j=1: access (a,b). j=2: (a,c). j=3: (a,d). i=1: j=2: (b,c). j=3: (b,d). i=2: j=3: (c,d). Total accesses: 6.
Indices: 0:x,1:y,2:z. i=0: j=1: (x,y). j=2: (x,z). i=1: j=2: (y,z). Total accesses: 3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Absolute Difference Between Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Calculate |a - b|, the non-negative difference between two values.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation computes the absolute difference between two numbers, which is fundamental to the problem. The intuition is that difference can be positive or negative depending on order, but for finding minimum gap, we care about magnitude, so we use absolute value to get a non-negative number. The algorithm is simple: given two numbers a and b, compute diff = |a - b|. In code, this can be done using abs(a - b) function or manually: if a >= b, diff = a - b; else diff = b - a. For the brute force approach, we apply this to each pair (arr[i], arr[j]). Time complexity is O(1) per computation. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L3 as it's the operation applied to each pair accessed by nested loops, and to L5 which covers basic arithmetic. Common mistakes include: not using absolute value (which could give negative differences and break min comparison), integer overflow for large numbers (though less common in Python), or misunderstanding that absolute difference is always non-negative. Real code pattern: diff = abs(arr[i] - arr[j]) or diff = arr[i] - arr[j] if arr[i] >= arr[j] else arr[j] - arr[i].

examples.trace: Compute |5 - 3| = |2| = 2. Alternatively: since 5 >= 3, diff = 5 - 3 = 2.
Compute |10 - 15| = |-5| = 5. Alternatively: since 10 < 15, diff = 15 - 10 = 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Arrays and Index Access
ladders.desc: Learn how arrays store elements and how to access them using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays, which are fundamental data structures for storing collections of elements. An array is a contiguous block of memory holding elements of the same type, accessible via indices starting from 0. The intuition is that arrays allow efficient access to any element by its position. For example, in array arr = [10, 20, 30], arr[0] gives 10, arr[1] gives 20, etc. The algorithm for access is straightforward: use the index inside square brackets. In the context of the brute force approach, we need to access pairs of elements like arr[i] and arr[j], so understanding zero-based indexing is crucial. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity for the array is O(n). This connects to L4 as array elements are the inputs for difference computation. Common mistakes include: using one-based indexing (e.g., thinking arr[1] is first element), accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., arr[3] for length 3), or confusing arrays with lists in Python (though similar for basic access). Real code pattern: arr = [1, 2, 3], first = arr[0], second = arr[1].

examples.trace: Array has indices: 0->5, 1->10, 2->15. Access arr[1]: go to index 1, retrieve value 10.

approaches.name: Sort then Linear Scan O(n log n)
approaches.summary: First sort the array if not already sorted, then iterate through adjacent elements to find the minimum difference. This is efficient and commonly used.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) if sorting modifies the array

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Minimum Difference Pair in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Given an array, find the pair of elements with the smallest absolute difference after sorting.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that in a sorted array, the minimum difference between any two elements must occur between adjacent elements. This is because sorting arranges elements in increasing order, so the closest values will be neighbors. If you consider non-adjacent elements, the difference will be larger due to intermediate values. The algorithm first sorts the array if it's not already sorted, which takes O(n log n) time. Then it performs a linear scan through the sorted array, comparing each element with its next neighbor and tracking the minimum absolute difference found. The space complexity is O(1) if sorting is done in-place, or O(n) if a new sorted array is created. This approach is optimal for this problem because any solution must at least examine differences, and sorting provides the structure needed to find the minimum efficiently. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like arrays with fewer than 2 elements, forgetting to take absolute values, or incorrectly assuming the array is pre-sorted. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the linear scan subroutine to compute differences after sorting.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array is [3, 1, 4, 2]. Step 2: Sort the array: [1, 2, 3, 4]. Step 3: Initialize min_diff = infinity. Step 4: Iterate through adjacent pairs: i=0: diff = |1-2| = 1, min_diff = 1. i=1: diff = |2-3| = 1, min_diff remains 1. i=2: diff = |3-4| = 1, min_diff remains 1. Step 5: Return min_diff = 1.

Step 1: Input array is [10, 5, 20, 15]. Step 2: Sort the array: [5, 10, 15, 20]. Step 3: Initialize min_diff = infinity. Step 4: Iterate through adjacent pairs: i=0: diff = |5-10| = 5, min_diff = 5. i=1: diff = |10-15| = 5, min_diff remains 5. i=2: diff = |15-20| = 5, min_diff remains 5. Step 5: Return min_diff = 5.

testCases.expected: Error: Array must have at least 2 elements

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Scan for Minimum Adjacent Difference
ladders.desc: Given a sorted array, find the minimum absolute difference between adjacent elements.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the full solution. The intuition is that in a sorted array, elements are arranged in non-decreasing order, so the closest values are always neighbors. By scanning through the array once and comparing each element with the next, we can efficiently find the smallest gap. The algorithm initializes a variable to track the minimum difference (starting with a large value like infinity). Then it loops from the first element to the second-to-last element, computing the absolute difference between the current element and the next element. If this difference is smaller than the current minimum, it updates the minimum. The time complexity is O(n) for the linear scan, and space complexity is O(1) as only a few variables are used. This feeds into L0 by providing the difference calculation after sorting. Common mistakes include accessing out-of-bounds indices by looping to the last element, not using absolute value for differences, or incorrectly initializing the minimum. The real code pattern involves a simple for-loop with difference computation.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input sorted array is [1, 2, 3, 4]. Step 2: Initialize min_diff = infinity. Step 3: Loop i from 0 to 2 (length-1): i=0: diff = |1-2| = 1, min_diff = 1. i=1: diff = |2-3| = 1, min_diff remains 1. i=2: diff = |3-4| = 1, min_diff remains 1. Step 4: Return min_diff = 1.

Step 1: Input sorted array is [5, 10, 15, 20]. Step 2: Initialize min_diff = infinity. Step 3: Loop i from 0 to 2: i=0: diff = |5-10| = 5, min_diff = 5. i=1: diff = |10-15| = 5, min_diff remains 5. i=2: diff = |15-20| = 5, min_diff remains 5. Step 4: Return min_diff = 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Why Minimum Difference is Between Adjacent Elements in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Understand the mathematical reasoning that the smallest difference must occur between neighboring elements after sorting.
ladders.explanation: This core logic explains why scanning adjacent pairs works. The intuition is based on the properties of sorted sequences. In a sorted array, elements are arranged such that a[i] ≤ a[i+1] for all i. Consider any two non-adjacent elements a[i] and a[j] with j > i+1. The difference |a[j] - a[i]| can be expressed as the sum of differences between consecutive elements from i to j: |a[j] - a[i]| = |(a[j] - a[j-1]) + (a[j-1] - a[j-2]) + ... + (a[i+1] - a[i])|. Since all these intermediate differences are non-negative (due to sorting), the total is at least as large as any individual adjacent difference. Therefore, the minimum difference cannot be smaller than the smallest adjacent difference. This insight reduces the problem from checking all O(n²) pairs to just O(n) adjacent pairs after sorting. The time to understand this is constant, and it connects to L1 by justifying the linear scan approach. Common mistakes include not considering absolute values or misunderstanding the triangle inequality in this context. The real pattern is a proof by contradiction or direct reasoning.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array is sorted: [1, 4, 7, 9]. Step 2: Adjacent differences: |1-4|=3, |4-7|=3, |7-9|=2. Minimum adjacent difference is 2. Step 3: Check non-adjacent pair 1 and 7: difference is 6, which is larger than 2. Step 4: Check pair 4 and 9: difference is 5, larger than 2. Step 5: This shows minimum difference is between adjacent elements 7 and 9.

Step 1: Array is sorted: [2, 5, 6, 8]. Step 2: Adjacent differences: |2-5|=3, |5-6|=1, |6-8|=2. Minimum adjacent difference is 1. Step 3: Check non-adjacent pair 2 and 6: difference is 4, larger than 1. Step 4: Check pair 5 and 8: difference is 3, larger than 1. Step 5: This confirms minimum is between adjacent 5 and 6.

testCases.expected: Minimum adjacent difference is 1, all non-adjacent differences are ≥1
Minimum adjacent difference is 2, all non-adjacent differences are ≥2
Minimum adjacent difference is 3, all non-adjacent differences are ≥3
Minimum adjacent difference is 2, all non-adjacent differences are ≥2
Minimum adjacent difference is 0, all non-adjacent differences are ≥0
Minimum adjacent difference is 10, all non-adjacent differences are ≥10
Minimum adjacent difference is 5, all non-adjacent differences are ≥5
Minimum adjacent difference is 3, all non-adjacent differences are ≥3
Minimum adjacent difference is 1, all non-adjacent differences are ≥1
Minimum adjacent difference is 50, all non-adjacent differences are ≥50

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting an Array
ladders.desc: Sort an array of integers in non-decreasing order using a standard sorting algorithm.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential because the full approach requires the array to be sorted before scanning. The intuition is that sorting organizes elements so that related operations (like finding minimum differences) become simpler. Common sorting algorithms include quicksort, mergesort, or built-in sorts, typically with O(n log n) time complexity. The algorithm depends on the chosen method; for example, mergesort divides the array recursively, sorts subarrays, and merges them. Space complexity can be O(n) for mergesort or O(log n) for quicksort. This connects to L2 by providing the sorted array needed for the core logic. Common mistakes include not handling duplicate values, using inefficient sorts like bubble sort (O(n²)), or modifying the original array unintentionally. The real code pattern often uses a language's built-in sort function for simplicity.

examples.trace: Step 1: Input array [3, 1, 4, 2]. Step 2: Apply sorting algorithm (e.g., mergesort): Divide into [3,1] and [4,2]. Sort [3,1] to [1,3], sort [4,2] to [2,4]. Merge [1,3] and [2,4]: compare 1 and 2, add 1; compare 3 and 2, add 2; compare 3 and 4, add 3; add 4. Result: [1,2,3,4].
Step 1: Input array [10, 5, 20, 15]. Step 2: Sort using quicksort: Choose pivot 10, partition into [5] and [20,15]. Sort [20,15] to [15,20]. Combine: [5] + [10] + [15,20] = [5,10,15,20].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compute Absolute Difference Between Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Calculate the absolute value of the difference between two integers.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental because the problem involves finding differences between elements. The intuition is that difference measures distance between numbers on the number line, and absolute value ensures it's non-negative, as we care about magnitude, not direction. The algorithm is straightforward: given two numbers a and b, compute |a - b|. This can be done by subtracting and taking the absolute value using a built-in function or by conditionally negating if the result is negative. Time and space complexity are O(1). This connects to L3 as it's used repeatedly in sorting comparisons and difference calculations. Common mistakes include forgetting absolute value (which could give negative differences), integer overflow for large numbers, or incorrect order of subtraction. The real code pattern is a simple expression or function call.

examples.trace: Step 1: Compute difference: 5 - 3 = 2. Step 2: Since 2 is non-negative, absolute value is 2. Step 3: Return 2.
Step 1: Compute difference: 2 - 7 = -5. Step 2: Take absolute value: |-5| = 5. Step 3: Return 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to access elements using indices.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept is crucial because the problem operates on arrays. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each identified by an index (usually starting at 0). The intuition is that arrays allow efficient access to elements by position, which is needed for sorting and scanning. For example, in an array arr of length n, arr[0] is the first element, arr[1] is the second, and arr[n-1] is the last. Time to access an element is O(1), and space is O(n) to store n elements. This connects to L4 as differences are computed between array elements accessed via indices. Common mistakes include accessing indices out of bounds (e.g., arr[n] for an array of size n), confusing 0-based indexing, or not understanding array mutability. The real pattern involves declaring arrays and using loops to traverse them.

examples.trace: Step 1: Array has 4 elements: indices 0 to 3. Step 2: arr[0] = 3, arr[1] = 7, arr[2] = 2, arr[3] = 9. Step 3: To loop through, use for i in range(4): access arr[i].
Step 1: Array has 3 elements: indices 0 to 2. Step 2: arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. Step 3: To access adjacent pairs, use arr[i] and arr[i+1] for i from 0 to 1.

approaches.name: Two Pointer O(n)
approaches.summary: Use two pointers to traverse the sorted array and compute differences between adjacent elements in a single pass. This is optimal for sorted input.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Find Minimum Difference Pair in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution using two-pointer traversal to find the pair with minimum absolute difference in a sorted array.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the pair of elements in a sorted array that have the smallest absolute difference. The two-pointer approach is optimal for sorted input because it leverages the ordering property to efficiently compare adjacent elements. The intuition is that in a sorted array, the minimum difference must occur between consecutive elements—if you consider any non-adjacent pair, there will be intermediate elements that create smaller differences. This reduces the problem to a single linear scan. The algorithm initializes a variable to track the minimum difference found (starting at infinity) and iterates through the array from index 1 to n-1. At each step, it computes the absolute difference between the current element and the previous element, updating the minimum if this difference is smaller. The time complexity is O(n) for the single pass, and space complexity is O(1) as only a few variables are used. This connects directly to L1 by implementing the complete traversal logic. Common mistakes include not handling edge cases like arrays with fewer than 2 elements (where no pair exists), or incorrectly initializing the minimum difference. Real code pattern: def min_diff_pair(arr): if len(arr) < 2: return None; min_diff = float('inf'); for i in range(1, len(arr)): diff = abs(arr[i] - arr[i-1]); if diff < min_diff: min_diff = diff; return min_diff.

examples.trace: Array: [1, 3, 6, 10, 15]. Initialize min_diff = inf. i=1: diff = |3-1| = 2, min_diff = 2. i=2: diff = |6-3| = 3, min_diff remains 2. i=3: diff = |10-6| = 4, min_diff remains 2. i=4: diff = |15-10| = 5, min_diff remains 2. Return 2.

Array: [5, 10, 20, 25, 30]. Initialize min_diff = inf. i=1: diff = |10-5| = 5, min_diff = 5. i=2: diff = |20-10| = 10, min_diff remains 5. i=3: diff = |25-20| = 5, min_diff remains 5. i=4: diff = |30-25| = 5, min_diff remains 5. Return 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traverse Array to Compute Adjacent Differences
ladders.desc: Implement a loop that iterates through a sorted array, calculating differences between consecutive elements.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the core traversal logic needed for the full problem. The intuition is that by moving through the array sequentially, we can access each element and its immediate neighbor to compute their difference. This is efficient because it requires only one pass, avoiding redundant comparisons. The algorithm involves initializing an index or pointer at the second element (since we need a pair), and for each position, subtracting the previous element from the current one to get the difference. We store these differences or process them immediately (e.g., finding the minimum). Time complexity is O(n) for the loop, and space is O(1) if we don't store all differences. This feeds into L0 by providing the mechanism to scan the array and gather necessary data. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop bounds (e.g., starting at index 0 instead of 1) or not ensuring the array is sorted beforehand. Real code pattern: def compute_adjacent_diffs(arr): diffs = []; for i in range(1, len(arr)): diff = arr[i] - arr[i-1]; diffs.append(diff); return diffs.

examples.trace: Array: [1, 4, 7, 9]. Initialize diffs = []. i=1: diff = 4-1 = 3, diffs = [3]. i=2: diff = 7-4 = 3, diffs = [3, 3]. i=3: diff = 9-7 = 2, diffs = [3, 3, 2]. Return [3, 3, 2].
Array: [10, 20, 30]. Initialize diffs = []. i=1: diff = 20-10 = 10, diffs = [10]. i=2: diff = 30-20 = 10, diffs = [10, 10]. Return [10, 10].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Find Minimum Value in a List
ladders.desc: Determine the smallest number in a collection of values, such as differences between elements.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is essential for identifying the minimum difference from a set of computed values. The intuition is that by comparing each value to a running minimum, we can efficiently find the smallest without sorting. The algorithm initializes a variable (min_val) to a very large number (or the first element), then iterates through the list, updating min_val whenever a smaller value is encountered. This is a linear scan, making it O(n) time and O(1) space. It connects to L1 by taking the list of differences and extracting the minimum, which is the goal in L0. Common mistakes include not initializing min_val properly (e.g., to 0, which might be smaller than all values) or forgetting to handle empty lists. Real code pattern: def find_min(values): if not values: return None; min_val = values[0]; for val in values: if val < min_val: min_val = val; return min_val.

examples.trace: List: [3, 1, 4, 2]. Initialize min_val = 3 (first element). val=3: 3 < 3? No, min_val=3. val=1: 1 < 3? Yes, min_val=1. val=4: 4 < 1? No, min_val=1. val=2: 2 < 1? No, min_val=1. Return 1.
List: [10, 20, 5, 15]. Initialize min_val = 10. val=10: 10 < 10? No, min_val=10. val=20: 20 < 10? No, min_val=10. val=5: 5 < 10? Yes, min_val=5. val=15: 15 < 5? No, min_val=5. Return 5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Compute Absolute Difference Between Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Calculate the non-negative difference between two integers, which is fundamental for comparing elements.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves finding the absolute difference, which is the distance between two numbers on the number line, always positive or zero. The intuition is that for a pair of elements, we care about how far apart they are, regardless of order, so we use absolute value. The algorithm subtracts one number from the other and takes the absolute value using a built-in function or manual check (if a < b, return b-a; else return a-b). This operation is O(1) time and space. It connects to L2 by providing the values to compare when finding the minimum difference. Common mistakes include forgetting to take the absolute value (resulting in negative differences) or mishandling large numbers that could overflow. Real code pattern: def abs_diff(a, b): return abs(a - b).

examples.trace: a=5, b=3. Compute difference: 5 - 3 = 2. Absolute value: |2| = 2. Return 2.
a=10, b=15. Compute difference: 10 - 15 = -5. Absolute value: |-5| = 5. Return 5.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Access Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieve values from an array using their positions, a fundamental skill for traversing data structures.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing elements in an array (or list) by their index, which is crucial for iterating and comparing values. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, allowing direct access via indices starting from 0. The algorithm simply uses the index to fetch the element, e.g., arr[i] returns the value at position i. This is O(1) time and space. It connects to L3 by providing the two numbers needed to compute their difference. Common mistakes include using out-of-bounds indices (e.g., accessing arr[len(arr)] which is invalid) or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: def get_element(arr, index): return arr[index].

examples.trace: Array: [10, 20, 30]. Index 1 corresponds to the second element. Access arr[1] = 20. Return 20.
Array: [5, 15, 25]. Index 0 corresponds to the first element. Access arr[0] = 5. Return 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is and how to use indices to refer to elements, a prerequisite for any traversal.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces arrays as ordered collections of elements, each accessible by a numerical index. The intuition is that arrays allow us to store multiple values in a single variable, making it easy to process data sequentially. An array is like a list of items where each item has a position number starting from 0. For example, in array [10, 20, 30], index 0 is 10, index 1 is 20, and index 2 is 30. To access an element, we use the index with bracket notation. This is fundamental because it enables us to retrieve values for operations like computing differences. Time and space are not applicable here as it's a conceptual understanding. It connects to L4 by providing the basis for element access. Common mistakes include thinking indices start at 1 or confusing arrays with other data structures. Real code pattern: arr = [10, 20, 30]; first_element = arr[0]; second_element = arr[1].

examples.trace: Array: [5, 10, 15]. Indices: 0 -> 5, 1 -> 10, 2 -> 15. Index 1 refers to the second element, which is 10.
Array: [2, 4, 6, 8]. Indices: 0 -> 2, 1 -> 4, 2 -> 6, 3 -> 8. Index 3 refers to the fourth element, which is 8.
